{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,

  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.5",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Which scope should the improvement prioritize? A) Focused copyediting only (grammar, concision, wording, minor sentence-level tweaks), or B) Substantive editorial work (reframing the contribution and thesis, checking logical consistency and evidence-claim alignment, and adjusting section/idea ordering, with light novelty and citation tuning).",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Refining the thesis and contribution framing can better foreground the estimand, assumptions, and practical significance of the findings.",
    "R1.2": "The chain from identification assumptions through estimation to conclusions should be checked for internal logical consistency.",
    "R1.3": "Claims about the ATE, confidence intervals, sensitivity bounds, and heterogeneous effects should align precisely with the reported analyses and numbers.",
    "R1.5": "Reordering and smoothing transitions can strengthen the abstract’s narrative from motivation to methods to results and implications.",
    "R1.6": "Sentence-level polishing will improve clarity, concision, and academic tone for readability.",
    "RX.1": "Improvements should preserve explicit uncertainty and assumptions (e.g., unconfoundedness, overlap) to avoid overstating confidence.",
    "RX.2": "Revisions must avoid overclaiming causal generality beyond the stated identification conditions and deployment population.",
    "RX.3": "Work will remain within textual improvement without adding new analyses or external data.",
    "RX.4": "Careful wording reduces risk of misinterpretation that could lead to misguided product or policy decisions.",
    "RX.5": "Any edits should be traceable to the provided content, maintaining clear provenance of claims and figures."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: Which scope should the improvement prioritize? A) Focused copyediting only (grammar, concision, wording, minor sentence-level tweaks), or B) Substantive editorial work (reframing the contribution and thesis, checking logical consistency and evidence-claim alignment, and adjusting section/idea ordering, with light novelty and citation tuning).

rationale.R1.1: Refining the thesis and contribution framing can better foreground the estimand, assumptions, and practical significance of the findings.
rationale.R1.2: The chain from identification assumptions through estimation to conclusions should be checked for internal logical consistency.
rationale.R1.3: Claims about the ATE, confidence intervals, sensitivity bounds, and heterogeneous effects should align precisely with the reported analyses and numbers.
rationale.R1.5: Reordering and smoothing transitions can strengthen the abstract’s narrative from motivation to methods to results and implications.
rationale.R1.6: Sentence-level polishing will improve clarity, concision, and academic tone for readability.
rationale.RX.1: Improvements should preserve explicit uncertainty and assumptions (e.g., unconfoundedness, overlap) to avoid overstating confidence.
rationale.RX.2: Revisions must avoid overclaiming causal generality beyond the stated identification conditions and deployment population.
rationale.RX.3: Work will remain within textual improvement without adding new analyses or external data.
rationale.RX.4: Careful wording reduces risk of misinterpretation that could lead to misguided product or policy decisions.
rationale.RX.5: Any edits should be traceable to the provided content, maintaining clear provenance of claims and figures.
